author: Sofia Martínez
title: Food Delivery Optimization: Increase Restaurant Order Volume 40%
description: More restaurant orders don't come from DoorDash tweaks. They come from profile optimization, photo refresh, and menu clarity. Real data inside.

sections.p: A pizzeria in Portland was getting crushed by delivery fees. They were on DoorDash, Uber Eats, and Grubhub but barely breaking even after commissions (15–30% per order). Here's what they didn't know: 73% of delivery order decisions happen in the first 8 seconds. They were losing before the customer even opened the menu. In 60 days, we overhauled their delivery presence across three platforms. Result: 38% order increase, $12,000 additional monthly revenue. The fix wasn't complicated. It was systematic.

sections.h2: Restaurant Profile Optimization Wins Before Menu Opens

sections.p: Your restaurant photo is your first impression. The pizzeria's profile photo was taken on a cloudy day with bad lighting. We swapped it for a high-quality shot of their signature pizza, shot with natural light. Clicks to their menu increased 22% that week. Then we optimized the bio: 'Fresh dough made daily. 45-minute average delivery.' Customers need to know what to expect. We also added operational tags — 'accepts tips,' 'tracked orders,' 'no contact delivery' — because customers search for these. They went from 5 tags to 12. Profile views jumped 31%.

list: Hero photo must be your best-seller dish (not your restaurant storefront)
Bio should include avg delivery time + unique value (e.g., 'gluten-free options', 'organic toppings')
Add all available tags for your service type
Update profile photo every 90 days to stay fresh in algorithm recommendations
Use 3–5 additional photos showing variety and quality

sections.p: The pizzeria's menu was overwhelming: 47 items. Customers couldn't decide. We restructured it: Top 8 bestsellers at the top. Then categories. Importantly, we priced differently per platform. DoorDash has slightly higher order values, so we positioned premium combos there. Uber Eats gets younger customers, so we bundled deals ('2 pizzas + wings + sides'). We also tested dynamic pricing: $2 off at lunch, $1 delivery credit after 9pm. Orders in those windows increased 47% and 34% respectively. The key: make top items obvious and give people reasons to order at off-peak times.

sections.quote: Restaurants with 8–12 bestsellers visible first convert 3x higher than restaurants with 40+ items listed equally. Scarcity and clarity beat abundance every time.

sections.p: The pizzeria had a 4.3-star rating with 87 reviews. That's solid but losing order volume to competitors at 4.6+ stars. We implemented a post-order follow-up text: 'Love your order? Drop a review' with a direct link. In 45 days they added 34 new reviews, mostly 5-stars. Rating climbed to 4.7. Orders from app search results increased 26% because the algorithm surfaces higher-rated restaurants first. We also flagged low ratings (< 4 stars) and asked customers to comment so we could respond publicly. Two negative reviews about wait times got owner responses within 2 hours: 'We're sorry. Here's a $5 credit.' Both reviews were then edited to 4 stars. That kind of reputation defense matters.

list: Send post-order review request text within 4 hours of delivery confirmation
Respond to all 1- and 2-star reviews within 24 hours — publicly
Aim for 4.6+ stars to stay competitive with premium restaurants in your category
Encourage photo reviews — restaurants with customer photos get 28% more orders

sections.p: The biggest mistake: promoting equally on all platforms. We analyzed order patterns and found lunch traffic was 60% Uber Eats, dinner was 50% DoorDash, late night was 70% Grubhub. We scheduled promotions by platform and daypart. Monday–Thursday lunch: $3 off on Uber Eats. Friday–Sunday dinner: Free wings with order $25+ on DoorDash. Sunday–Thursday 10pm+: 15% off Grubhub. This targeted approach eliminated wasted discounting. They ran 8 weekly promotions (2 per platform) instead of blasting one site-wide offer. Result: 19% higher AOV and 40% order lift without increasing ad spend.
